messages.content: paraphrase 2:33am-YA called TS and stated she is having a rough week and not feeling the best and have some relationship drama and in a really bad mindset and don’t want to be alone.  No SI but is having a little bit of self-harm thoughts.  Interpersonal relationship drama triggered tonight’s episode.  TS discussed with CS who recommended seeing if YA had a friend she could reach out to for the next couple of hours until her LSS was on shift.  2:44am-TS reached back out to YA to let her know the ETA would be over an hour and asked her if she had a friend that she could reach out to or if she could talk to her grandma until her LSS came on.  She stated she was willing to reach out to a friend and then reach out to LSS.

At 2:33 AM, YA contacted TS, expressing that she was going through a challenging week, feeling down with some issues in her relationship, and struggling with a negative mindset. She mentioned not wanting to be alone and having fleeting thoughts of self-harm, though she had no intentions of suicide. The difficulties in her personal relationships had exacerbated her distress. TS spoke with CS about YA's situation, and they suggested finding a friend for YA to connect with until her LSS could take over. By 2:44 AM, TS followed up with YA, informing her that it would take more than an hour for her LSS to be available. TS inquired if YA could reach out to a friend or speak with her grandmother in the meantime. YA agreed to contact a friend and then get in touch with her LSS.

discuss the findings in this: The rate of suicide among public safety officers is higher in comparison to that of the
U.S. general population. An extensive study was carried out utilizing data extracted from death
certificates originating from 26 states within the United States. The study consisted of states in
the West, Northeast, Midwest, and South. The findings suggest that police officers have a
considerably higher rate of suicide-related mortality compared to the non-police officer study
group. Specifically, police officers have a 54% higher likelihood of dying by suicide compared
to individuals in other occupations (Violanti & Steege, 2021). There may be many job-related
factors that contribute to poor mental health and suicidality in police officers. The career is
related with several demands, including organizational, operational, physical, and administrative
factors (Edwards et al., 2021; McCarty et al., 2019; Violanti, 2017). Police personnel have
numerous other challenges that lead to their compromised mental well-being. Individuals in this
population encounter a range of challenges, including substance use, divorce, feelings of despair,
anxiety, ptsd, and burnout. To enhance comprehension regarding strategies intended for
mitigating the prevalence of mental illness and suicide within the police force, it would be
advantageous to acquire knowledge pertaining to the psychological elements that exert the most
U.S. LAW ENFORCEMENT OFFICER SUICIDE 91
adverse influence on the overall well-being of police officers. RQ1 included the synthesis of 70
journal articles.
Deterioration of Mental Well-being
The initial prominent theme identified in the analysis of 17 articles revealed a significant
prevalence of stress and burnout among law enforcement personnel. The potential etiology of
impaired mental well-being among law enforcement officers can be attributed to various factors,
including the stressors inherent in their professional responsibilities, utilization of maladaptive
coping mechanisms, inadequate equilibrium between work and personal life, familial discord,
and the experience of burnout. In certain circumstances, police officers may experience
heightened distress that could potentially lead to self-harm or suicidal ideation (Baker et al.,
2023).
The job stress encountered by officers can certainly be attributed to multiple factors in the
external work environment, including adverse contacts with community members and increased
anxiety stemming from the nature of the job (Paoline & Gau, 2023). Stressors ranging from
increased work demands to inadequate support significantly contribute to the stress experienced
by police officers, impacting their mental and physical health and potentially leading to burnout
(Raju et al., 2020; Queirós et al., 2020). The findings of research conducted on three urban police
agencies in Texas indicate that organizational stressors play a substantial role in the development
of anger, low-mood, and burnout (Bishopp et al., 2019). Another study demonstrated that
organizational stress is associated with emotional fatigue, which serves as an indicator of burnout
(McCarty et al., 2019). The prevalence of suboptimal mental well-being within the police force
is often accompanied by the adoption of maladaptive coping mechanisms, inadequate
equilibrium between work and personal life, familial discord, and the onset of burnout (Griffin &
U.S. LAW ENFORCEMENT OFFICER SUICIDE 92
Sun, 2017). According to a study conducted by Allison et al., (2019), a significant number of
police officers were found to be experiencing feelings of hopelessness, depression, and stress.
Research has demonstrated that stress and burnout contributed to many police officers ending
their own lives (Violanti et al., 2018; Ramchand et al., 2018). There exists a correlation between
the demands inherent in the police profession and many negative mental health outcomes,
including but not limited to anxiety, depression, elevated stress levels, burnout, and instances of
suicide (Grant et al., 2019; Violanti et al., 2018). Extensive scholarly investigations pertaining to
burnout among police officers have revealed notable instances of exhaustion and
depersonalization, alongside established associations between burnout and mental health
concerns (McCarty et al., 2019; Talavera-Velasco et al., 2018).
The psychological health of the police officers seemed to be compromised due to their
limited leisure time outside of work. The most prominent operational stressors were shown to be
associated with temporal factors, health-related factors, or a combination of both. The
organizational stressors that induce the highest levels of stress are primarily related to job
demands and impartiality in leadership, specifically pertaining to the disparate distribution of job
duties and the perception of inconsistent application of rules among officers. Operational stresses
predominantly include events that occur in the field, including but not limited to responding to
calls for assistance and incidents involving law enforcement officers discharging their firearms.
Organizational stresses involve factors that are within the realm of managerial flexibility and
decision-making, including hours of work, equipment, and resources (Edwards et al., 2021).
The police service is often linked to significant expenses resulting from absenteeism and
staff turnover, which are attributed to the presence of stress and burnout. There exists a
significant association between burnout and symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder, which
U.S. LAW ENFORCEMENT OFFICER SUICIDE 93
can be attributed to both direct and indirect pathways, primarily including the process of
rumination over the traumatic events that have been encountered. The results suggest that police
officers who are encountering burnout and engage in rumination on past traumatic experiences
are at a heightened risk of developing post-traumatic stress disorder. Law enforcement personnel
who are afflicted by burnout exhibit diminished levels of energy to effectively cope with
distressing stimuli, rendering them more susceptible to developing post-traumatic stress disorder
(Ogińska-Bulik & Juczyński, 2021).
The Significance of Organizational Stressors
The analysis of 15 studies uncovered a second significant theme, which indicated that
organizational stressors exerted a greater influence on the mental well-being of police officers
compared to other types of stressors. The origins of emotional distress are comparable for both
police officers and non-operational police personnel, thereby demonstrating that suffering does
not solely arise from police operations (Kerswell et al. 2020). Police personnel view
organizational stressors as overbearing, unwarranted, and inevitable. The factors encompassed in
this category consist of insufficient assistance, excessive workloads, shiftwork, court mandated
days, interpersonal disputes with other officers or superiors, limited resources, time constraints,
and an excessively bureaucratic structure that penalizes and tenaciously supervises individuals.
These factors have the potential to contribute to and/or worsen symptoms of anxiety and
depression, burnout, post-traumatic stress disorder, and thoughts of suicide.
A substantial body of research suggests that the operational stressors formerly believed to
have the most adverse effects on mental well-being are not the primary stressors in the context of
a law enforcement career (Gershon et al., 2009; Shane 2010; Trinker et al., 2016). Many police
officers often find themselves overwhelmed by the organizational challenges posed by strenuous
U.S. LAW ENFORCEMENT OFFICER SUICIDE 94
job schedules, bureaucratic processes, and constant policy modifications. nepotism, government
systems and processes, and styles, are perceived to have a greater influence on the mental well-
being of officers (Chopko et al., 2021; Phythian et al.,2021; Baker et al., 2020;). Government
procedural requirements, the persistent need to validate one's worth to the organization, disparate
application of regulations, and inadequate staffing levels are prominent sources of stress
experienced by police officers (Chopko et al., 2021). The findings of a meta-analysis examining
the relationship between stresses experienced by law enforcement personnel and their health
outcomes revealed that frequent exposure to stressors within the organizational context was
significantly linked to adverse physical and mental health (Goh et al., 2015). According to
Edwards et al., 2021, the primary factor contributing to stress was the insufficiency of personnel.
Other prominent stressors in the workplace include high workload, conflicts with colleagues and
supervisors, limited resources, and a bureaucratic organizational structure characterized by strict
management and punitive measures towards police personnel (Moreno et al., 2016). Numerous
studies repeatedly demonstrate that elevated levels of organizational stress are causally linked to
diminished career satisfaction, thereby leading to turnover and insufficient staffing (McCreary &
Thompson, 2006; Nisar & Rasheed, 2020; Wolfe & Lawson, 2020).
Police officers are individuals who work in a professional capacity and are regularly
exposed to various situations that may involve encountering challenging circumstances. These
situations often entail continued exposure to instances of distress, potential risks to their personal
safety as well as that of their colleagues, the utilization of weapons, participation in emergency
operations, and exposure to traumatic events, including instances of death (Violanti et al., 2017;
Violanti et al., 2019; Purba & Demou 2019). When considering law enforcement, one typically
contemplates the inherent risks associated with the role, including the apprehension and arrest of
U.S. LAW ENFORCEMENT OFFICER SUICIDE 95
those deemed dangerous, exposure to perilous situations on roadways, engagement in armed
conflicts with potentially lethal suspects, and involvement in high-speed pursuits.
However, it is the less apparent stressors that have demonstrated great influence on the
mental well-being of police officers. The stressors that may not be readily apparent to individuals
who are not employed as police officers incorporate various organizational characteristics, such
as long hours, inadequate resources, social support, culture, leadership, and bureaucracy. The
presence of certain organizational elements may give rise to adverse consequences for
individuals, such as the development of stigmatized perceptions while seeking support services,
the experience of anxiety and depressive symptoms, the occurrence of burnout, the manifestation
of post-traumatic stress disorder, and the emergence of suicide ideation, among other potential
outcomes

The research indicates that public safety officers, specifically police officers, have a significantly higher rate of suicide than the general population in the U.S., with police officers being 54% more likely to die by suicide. This heightened risk is linked to various job-related stressors, including organizational, operational, physical, and administrative challenges. Factors contributing to poor mental health and increased suicidality in police officers are numerous and include substance use, divorce, despair, anxiety, PTSD, and burnout.

Key findings include a notable prevalence of stress and burnout among law enforcement personnel, attributed to the inherent stressors of their professional duties, maladaptive coping mechanisms, poor work-life balance, family discord, and burnout. These stressors not only come from the external pressures of their work environment, such as community interactions and the nature of their duties but also from organizational stressors like increased work demands and lack of support. A critical insight from the research is that organizational stressors have a more significant impact on police officers' mental health than operational stressors. This includes factors such as inadequate support, excessive workloads, shiftwork, and a bureaucratic system that fails to provide adequate resources while exerting excessive supervision.

The analysis revealed that police services face substantial costs due to absenteeism and staff turnover resulting from stress and burnout. There's a strong association between burnout and PTSD, suggesting that officers who ruminate on past traumatic events are at an increased risk of PTSD. Among the key organizational stressors identified, insufficient staffing, high workloads, and bureaucratic structures were prominent, leading to diminished job satisfaction and contributing to staff turnover.

Furthermore, the analysis emphasizes that the operational risks typically associated with police work, such as handling dangerous situations or engaging with armed suspects, are not the only sources of stress. Organizational issues like long hours, inadequate resources, lack of social support, and bureaucracy significantly impact officers' mental well-being. The presence of these stressors can lead to anxiety, depression, burnout, PTSD, and suicidal ideation, highlighting the need for interventions that address both the visible and less apparent challenges police officers face.

Discusss the findings: The examination of eight articles revealed an additional noteworthy theme, suggesting that the prevalence of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) within the police profession exceeds that of the general population. The phenomenon of police stress and trauma is characterized by its intricate structure, often encompassing regular, long-term exposure to stressors inherent in the profession, as well as potentially traumatic events. Police officers frequently encounter potentially stressful situations and have elevated levels of stress. Police officers frequently experience numerous situations that have the potential to result in the development of post-traumatic stress disorder. Post-traumatic stress disorder is characterized by a range of symptoms, including the reliving of traumatic events, avoidance of trauma-related stimuli, negative cognitive patterns, mood disturbances, as well as heightened arousal, which may show as aggression, sleep disturbances, recklessness, or self-destructive behaviors (American Psychiatric Association, 2013). 
	The traumatic situations generally acknowledged entail observing a person enduring severe injuries, sustaining severe injuries oneself, encountering catastrophic events, suffering physical attack, witnessing a colleague in law enforcement sustaining injuries, witnessing the death of an individual, being subjected to threats using firearms, or being targeted with gunfire (Hartley et al., 2014; Papazoglou, & Tuttle, 2018; Regehr, 2019). The research  A study of 434 police officers found that an astounding 68% screened positive for symptoms of PTSD (Jetelina et al., 2020). The rate of PTSD among the general population is 4.4% (Powell-Chandler, 2020). In another study, law enforcement officers were found to have elevated levels of depression, anxiety, PTSD, and suicide in comparison to the broader population (Dawson, 2019). In separate studies, law enforcement officers exhibit a higher prevalence of heart disease, PTSD, and suicide compared to the general population (Hartley et al., 2011; Spence & Drake, 2021). Similarly, Collazo (2020) found police officers to be at an elevated risk of getting post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), experiencing suicide thoughts, and engaging in substance use as compared to individuals in the general population. Law enforcement officers are more susceptible to a wide range of potentially traumatic incidents as compared to other groups which has led to the high rate of PTSD (Martinaki et al., 2021).
	In comparison to other cohorts, individuals employed in the field of law enforcement exhibit a heightened susceptibility to a diverse range of potentially traumatic incidents. Consequently, there has been a notable escalation in the contingent susceptibility to experiencing compassion fatigue, moral injury, and post-traumatic stress disorder (Papazoglou & Tuttle, 2018). Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) has been seen to impact approximately 20% of the law enforcement personnel in the United States (Hartley et al., 2013). The elevated prevalence of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) can be attributed, in part, to the fact that most police officers are exposed to a minimum of five stressful situations annually (Hartely et al., 2014). The experience of encountering death emerged as the most significant predictor for the manifestation of symptoms related to post-traumatic stress disorder, including intrusive traumatic memories and avoidance behaviors. Individuals who have been exposed to death may exhibit signs of hyperarousal associated with post-traumatic stress disorder. The heightened exposure to traumatic experiences may lead to an increased state of hypervigilance among law enforcement officers during the execution of their professional responsibilities.
Discussion Research Question One
There are many factors that contribute to the stress level of law enforcement officers. As a result, the stress level among police officers is substantial. There are significant associations of stress and burnout among the law enforcement population. Burnout ensues because of workplace stress that has not been effectively addressed. The evidence suggests that police officers need to improve their ability to mitigate stress and reduce burnout. The stress that derives from performing operational tasks would be difficult to prevent given the nature of the dangerous profession. The mitigation of stress arising from the execution of operational activities within a hazardous profession is a challenging endeavor due to the fundamental nature of the occupation. The high rate of organizational stress suggests that overworking, a poor work-life balance, and supervisory leadership that is not conducive to a supportive environment significantly impacts police officer stress. Nevertheless, it may be possible to ease the organizational stress associated with the profession through modifications in work hours, working environment, scheduling, and management styles. 
One fundamental principle posited by the theory of constructed emotion is that the brain dynamically generates emotions in real-time, drawing upon past experiences and anticipations of future events. Also, the theory of constructed emotion implies that one's own internal processes, rather than external factors, are responsible for the development of stress. There is a possibility that the recurrent encounter with organizational stress is consistent with the proposition that police officers, through repeated exposure to job-related stress, may rely on prior difficult experiences and predict forthcoming organizational stress. When police officers are indulged in poor work environment, long irregular hours, poor work-life balance, and difficult leadership styles without being involved in enough self-care the stressors would be used to create sensations from an unbalanced body budget (Barrett, 2016). The phenomenon of regulating the energy requirements of the brain and body, known as allostasis, operates on the principle that the brain predicts and endeavors to fulfill bodily desires in advance. This proactive approach is deemed more efficient than reacting to energy demands retrospectively. Police officers are more susceptible to experiencing distal health problems due to PTSD compared to individuals in less demanding professions. Several illnesses are distinguished by allostatic dysregulation, which is further aggravated by the work patterns of law enforcement personnel that disturb the internal clock on the brain.

The summary of findings from the examined articles illuminates the concerning rate of Post-traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) amongst police officers, displaying a prevalence far surpassing that of the general population. This elevated rate of PTSD in law enforcement is attributed to their frequent encounters with stress-inducing and potentially traumatic events as part of their routine duties. 

Key Insights Include:

1. **Prevalence and Impact of PTSD in Police Officers**: Approximately 68% of surveyed police officers displayed symptoms of PTSD, a stark contrast to the 4.4% prevalence rate within the general population. Factors such as witnessing severe injuries, personal injury, exposure to traumatic events, and threats involving firearms contribute significantly to this heightened incidence. The stress and trauma associated with police work not only involve high exposure to dangerous and distressing situations but also encompass the intricate nature of these encounters which are of a recurring and long-term nature.

2. **Physical and Mental Health Outcomes**: The ramifications of such extensive and profound exposure to traumatic events extend beyond PTSD, inclusive of increased levels of depression, anxiety, heart disease, and notably, a heightened risk of suicide. This reveals a critical aspect of law enforcement as a profession wherein officers are perpetually at risk of both mental and physical health issues, underlining the urgency for targeted interventions and support mechanisms.

3. **Organizational Stress and Its Role**: The examination also underscores the significant aspect of organizational stress, originating from work overload, poor work-life balance, and, at times, a lack of supportive leadership. These factors collectively exacerbate the stress encountered from operational activities, suggesting that reforms in work schedules, environment, and managerial approaches could potentially mitigate the adverse effects of such stressors.

4. **Theoretical Perspectives on Stress and PTSD**: The findings suggest an alignment with the theory of constructed emotion, pointing towards the brain's dynamic process of generating emotions based on past experiences and future anticipations. In the context of police work, repeated exposure to stressors could predispose officers to anticipatory stress responses, exacerbating the risk of PTSD and other stress-related conditions. This aligns with the concepts of allostasis and allostatic load, indicating that the chronic stress and irregular work patterns prevalent in law enforcement could disrupt the body's natural regulatory processes, leading to long-term health implications.

5. **Implications for Intervention and Support**: Given the elevated rates of PTSD and the broad spectrum of stress-related issues among police officers, there are clear indications for the need of comprehensive support systems tailored to address both the psychological and organizational facets contributing to stress and trauma in law enforcement. This includes promoting mental health literacy, providing accessible mental health services, implementing preventative stress management training, and considering organizational reforms aimed at reducing stressors.

In conclusion, the recurrent exposure to traumatic events, coupled with organizational stress challenges, significantly elevates the risk of PTSD among police officers, demanding urgent attention to the mental health and well-being of law enforcement personnel. Future strategies should aim at both mitigating the sources of stress inherent in police work and enhancing the support structures available to officers navigating the psychological repercussions of their profession.

The investigation into the mental health challenges faced by public safety officers, particularly police officers, reveals a markedly higher incidence of suicide within this demographic compared to the U.S. general population. Police officers are found to be 54% more susceptible to suicide, a statistic that underscores the severe impact of job-related stressors encompassing organizational, operational, physical, and administrative pressures. Contributory factors to the compromised mental health and escalated risk of suicidality among police officers are diverse, spanning substance abuse, marital dissolution, despair, anxiety, Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD), and occupational burnout.

The analysis uncovers a pronounced presence of stress and burnout within the law enforcement community, primarily attributed to the stressors innate to their roles, maladaptive coping strategies, imbalance between professional and personal life, familial strife, and resultant burnout. These stressors emerge not only from the external pressures related to their work environment and interactions but also from organizational stressors including, but not limited to, increased workload demands and insufficient support infrastructure. A pivotal insight from this research indicates that organizational stressors significantly jeopardize police officers' mental health more than operational stressors do, entailing inadequate support, excessive workloads, shift work, and a bureaucratic regime that curtails resource availability while imposing stringent oversight.

The findings also illuminate the substantial financial and operational toll on police services due to stress and burnout-induced absenteeism and staff turnover. There exists a strong correlation between burnout and PTSD, suggesting officers persistently reflecting on past traumatic occurrences are prone to PTSD. Identified key organizational stressors include insufficient staffing, overwhelming workloads, and bureaucratic infrastructures, factors that erode job satisfaction and catalyze staff turnover.

Moreover, the investigative outcomes divulge that stressors intrinsic to police work—such as managing perilous situations or contending with armed adversaries—aren't the sole sources of stress. Organizational nuances like extended work hours, inadequate resources, deficient social support, and cumbersome bureaucracy substantially deteriorate officers’ mental well-being. The presence of these stressors precipitates anxiety, depression, burnout, PTSD, and suicidal ideation, accentuating the necessity for interventions that tackle the evident and more latent challenges faced by police officers.

Expanding upon these findings, the research presents alarming statistics on the prevalence of Post-traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) amongst police officers, showcasing a significant overshoot compared to the general population. This amplified rate of PTSD in law enforcement is attributed to their routine exposure to stress-inducing and potentially traumatic episodes.

Crucial insights include:

1. **Prevalence and Impact of PTSD in Police Officers**: Research indicated that an overwhelming 68% of surveyed police officers exhibited symptoms of PTSD, starkly contrasting with a 4.4% prevalence rate within the general population. A myriad of factors significantly contributing to this spike includes witnessing or experiencing severe injuries, exposure to traumatic events, and threats involving firearms.
2. **Physical and Mental Health Outcomes**: The extensive exposure to traumatic events ushers beyond PTSD, encompassing increased depression, anxiety, heart disease, and notably, an augmented suicide risk. These findings highlight a critical aspect of the law enforcement profession, wherein officers are in constant jeopardy of mental and physical health deteriorations, underscoring an urgent call for targeted support and intervention mechanisms.
3. **Organizational Stress and Its Role**: The synthesis further elucidates the profound impact of organizational stress—stemming from work overload, poor work-life synergy, and occasionally, unsupportive leadership—amplifying the stress derived from operational duties. Suggestive reforms in work schedules, environments, and management methodologies could potentially alleviate these stressors' adverse effects.
4. **Theoretical Perspectives on Stress and PTSD**: The alignment with the theory of constructed emotion illustrates the brain's dynamic emotion generation process, influenced by previous experiences and future anticipations. In policing contexts, recurrent stressor exposure predisposes officers to anticipatory stress responses, magnifying PTSD and other stress-related disorder risks. This notion harmonizes with allostasis and allostatic load principles, highlighting how chronic stress and irregular work patterns in law enforcement may disrupt natural regulatory processes, leading to enduring health implications.
5. **Implications for Intervention and Support**: The pronounced rates of PTSD and a spectrum of stress-related conditions among officers underscore the imperative for comprehensive support systems, addressing both psychological and organizational determinants of stress and trauma in law enforcement. This entails fostering mental health literacy, ensuring accessible mental health services, instituting preventive stress management training, and contemplating organizational reforms to diminish stressors.

In summation, the recurrent traumatic exposure, coupled with organizational stress challenges, significantly escalates the PTSD risk among police officers, warranting immediate attention to their mental health and well-being. Future strategies should concurrently aim at alleviating inherent occupational stress sources and enhancing support structures for officers grappling with the psychological aftermath of their profession.

discuss the findings: Due to the accumulation of traumatic experiences and the high incidence of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and other related disorders, first responders are widely regarded as a suitable population for routine mental health screening. The promotion of screening is frequently advocated as a strategy to support the maintenance of a mentally sound work environment. Police agencies engage in screening procedures with the aim of identifying initial signs of mental disorders, thereby facilitating timely mental health services. Police departments conduct pre-employment testing to decrease the likelihood of recruiting individuals who may exhibit vulnerability to prospective mental health issues. With the presentation of problematic behaviors, it may be necessary for experienced law enforcement personnel to undergo a fitness for duty evaluation.   Notwithstanding the deployment of screening tools, a substantial prevalence of mental health concerns persists among police officers. RQ 2 included the analysis of 18 articles. 
MMPI-2 Screening Tool
The primary theme that emerged from the analysis of 9 articles is that the Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory-2 (MMPI-2) can serve as a predictive tool for future instances of police officer misconduct. The MMPI-2 scales are designed to assess various factors that have been identified through research as predictors of mental illness and psychopathology (Marshall et al., 2020a). A study examined the correlation between MMPI-2 scores and the specific behaviors that led to the termination of employment among a large cohort of newly recruited individuals in the field of law enforcement. The research findings indicated that specific scores on the MMPI-2 were correlated with behaviors that were not conducive to achieving effective employment within the police force (Loya et al., 2022). A separate study revealed that police officers who have been accused of engaging in excessive force and/or racist behavior exhibit higher K scores and lower F scores in comparison to their counterparts within the sampled population of police officers (Borgagna, 2022). Additional studies support the proposition that the MMPI-2 has a high degree of accuracy in predicting prospective behavioral issues among police officers, which involve mental, ethical, and performance-related challenges (Sellbom et al., 2007; Weiss et al., 2013). Two additional studies concluded that no significant correlation existed between MMPI-2 scores and mental health problems (Marshall et al., 2020b; Marshall et al., 2021).
The findings of one study have established that the utilization of Machine Learning (ML) techniques with the MMPI-2 yields consistent and dependable results in accurately categorized and projecting an individual's thoughts of suicide and previous suicide attempts (Kim et al., 2021b). The presence of an instrument that exhibits high accuracy in predicting mental health outcomes is advantageous; yet, exercising discretion in the selection process may pose significant risks, particularly in relation to the advancement of cadets to subsequent phases of the recruiting process. Another study that focuses on the creation of a novel scale within the MMPI-2 framework. The primary objective of this scale is to detect potential suicide risk among individuals undergoing examination, particularly those who may exhibit hesitancy in endorsing items that directly address explicit suicidal thoughts or behaviors. The findings of this study suggest that the metric employed is a reliable indicator of the likelihood of suicide (Nichols et al., 2021). 
Despite the premise of accurately anticipating potential concerning conduct, there is a tendency to disregard or downplay negative findings in order to advance the cadet to the subsequent stage of the recruitment process (Inwald & Thompson, 2021). In the preceding year, a number of departments have also been relaxing their rules, leading to the selection of individuals who may possess doubtful qualifications or exhibit mental unsuitability (Inwald & Thompson, 2021). Therefore, the efficacy of the screening measures is contingent upon the subsequent adherence to the obtained results. 
MMPI-2-Restructured Form Screening Tool
A major theme that emerged from the analysis of 9 articles over the span of 2014 to 2022 is that the Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory-2 Restructured Form (MMPI-2-RF) can successfully predict future police officer behavior and vulnerability to emotional problems. The MMPI-2-RF is a comprehensive self-report inventory designed to evaluate a wide range of contemporary psychopathological symptoms and personality traits. The MMPI-2-RF elements are grouped into various scales that assess for clinical significance. The instrument measures criteria by which police candidates demonstrate actions that have the potential to hinder their effectiveness in fulfilling their professional duties.   One study identified the scores related to thought dysfunction are correlated with feelings of isolation and a lack of focus on achieving goals (Roberts et al., 2018).
	Psychopathology can profoundly modify personality, either in a positive or negative manner. For instance, it is possible that enduring a severe mental illness, could permanently influence one's cognitive, emotional, and interpersonal tendencies (Widiger, 2011). This modification of personality does not necessarily indicate just a persistent but mild expression of psychopathology. It may also signify the emergence of new personality traits because of the presence or encounter of psychopathology. The study findings suggest that the MMPI-2-RF spectrum scales show promise validity in evaluating psychopathology associated to personality disorders, in terms of criterion, concept, and incremental measures (Brown & Sellbom, 2020; Sellbom, 2019). It has been established that potential hazardous conduct in law enforcement professionals may be anticipated by analyzing their previous behaviors (Corey et al., 2018; Sellbom et al., 2021). Another study was implemented to explore the correlations between MMPI-2-RF and biodata, as well as post-hire metrics for performance, in a specific group of officers. They were deemed psychologically fit, were later employed, and had accessible records of their performance after being hired. The results suggest that combining MMPI-2-RF scores with biodata can enhance the accuracy of predicting undesirable outcomes (Corey et al., 2018). 
	The MMPI-2-RF had a revision and was subsequently renamed as MMPI-3 (Ben-Porath & Tellegen, 2020). The assessment has more than 300 items that are evaluated using numerous validity measures to detect inconsistent responses, exaggerated reporting, minimized reporting, and other relevant scales. In line with the findings of previous studies on the MMPI-2-RF, a recent investigation revealed a significant correlation between mmpi-3 MMPI-3 scores and the post-10 dimensions (Whitman et al., 2021). The research derived exhibited convergent validity for assessing law enforcement officer candidates. However, some contend that the MMPI-2-RF is not without its shortcomings (Balthrop 2018; Corey & Ben-Porath, 2014). In situations when individuals are being assessed for employment, the deliberate deception of symptoms poses a significant challenge, especially when it involves downplaying, omitting, or minimizing pathological traits, emotions, or behavioral abnormalities. An inappropriate cadet may be erroneously sworn in due to false or inaccurate psychiatric reporting (Balthrop 2018; Corey & Ben-Porath, 2014). 
MMPI-3 Screening Tool
	An important finding derived from the examination of 9 articles pertaining to the MMPI-3 and pre-employment screening is that it serves as a valuable instrument for assessing traits that are very likely to result in unfavorable consequences in police performance.  The Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory (MMPI) has consistently been one of the most extensively utilized psychological instruments in the assessment of police officer applicants during pre-hire screenings. The newest version, the MMPI-3, is a recently revised iteration of the MMPI-2-RF (Ben-Porath & Tellegen, 2020a,b). The MMPI-3 is inclusive of the POST-10 scales created by The California Commission on Peace Officer standards and Training (Spillberg & Corey, 2020).
The California Commission on Peace Officer standards and Training (Spilberg & Corey, 2020) mandates rigorous requirements that necessitate the evaluation of all recruits for law enforcement across various parameters: Emotional Regulation and Stress Tolerance, Avoidance of Substance Use and Other Risk-Taking Behavior, Impulse Control and Attention to Safety, Assertiveness, Social Competence, Teamwork, Decision Making and Judgment, Adaptability and Flexibility, Conscientiousness and Dependability, and Integrity and Ethics. A study examined the correlations between issues identified in pre-hire screenings of public safety candidates using the MMPI-3 assessment. The MMPI-3 scores showed regular and substantial associations with the POST-10 aspects. 
Previous studies have demonstrated that law enforcement professionals endure elevated levels of stress compared to individuals in most other professions and frequently suffer significant mental health issues (Price, 2017). The initial two peer-reviewed research focused on the examination of the MMPI-3 and its application in pre-employment screening for police officer candidates. These studies assessed the accuracy and practicality of using the MMPI-3 in this context (Whitman et al., 2021; Whitman et al., 2022). One study found that the MMPI-3 was not only effective in assessing police candidates but was also unable to find any indication to suggest significant or widespread differences in emotional or interpersonal issues that can be attributed to being subjected to occupational-related stressors (Whitman et al.,2022). The MMPI-3 PCIR identified 16 thresholds that were linked to a higher likelihood of experiencing difficulties in at least part of the POST-10 dimensions (Whitman et al., 2021).  These findings of the research support the criterion validity of MMPI-3 scores in the pre-hire screening of police officers (Whitman et al., 2021).  
A recent study contributes to the existing body of literature on the interpretation of MMPI-3 results in pre-hire psychological examinations. It provides information on the frequencies of interpretable examinations, as well as the average scores and validity coefficients for MMPI-3 scores in a sample that includes nearly 2,000 public safety candidates. The findings confirmed the capacity to make accurate conclusions based on MMPI-3 scores across several groups, including police officers, correctional officers, dispatchers, firefighters, and male and female police officer applicants (Whitman et al., 2023). A supplementary analysis of 527 male and female police candidates revealed that there is no substantial disparity in the effectiveness of the MMPI-3 assessment tool between male and female candidates (Talerico et al., 2024).  
The MMPI-3 can detect both under reporting and over reporting as well. Research has demonstrated that both the MMPI-2 RF and MMPI-3 incorporate mechanisms to address the issue of deliberate underreporting within this specific group. This is advantageous, as the majority of applicants desire to present themselves in the most favorable light. The L-r and K-r validity measures have demonstrated use in detecting test takers who intentionally provide inaccurate or incomplete responses (Brown & Sellbom, 2019; Detrick & Chibnall, 2014; Tarescavage, Brewster, et al., 2015; Tarescavage, Fischler, et al., 2015). Another study supports the use of MMPI-3 and has determined that it's possible to detect response bias related to the over reporting of symptoms (Reeves et al., 2022).  
Discussion Research Question Two
Advocates frequently recommend mental health screening as a method to support the maintenance of a psychologically sound work environment. Personality tests are frequently conducted to screen and evaluate the mental well-being of prospective police cadets. Personality assessments are frequently carried out throughout the recruitment process for police officers. The MMPI-2 is extensively used in the process of selecting applicants. It has been employed to project the applicants who are most probable to excel in the role and do not possess personality features that are more vulnerable to mental illness. 
The MMPI-2-RF is another widely utilized mental health screening tool for prospective police officers. The results of the evidence synthesis revealed that the MMPI-2-RF possesses the capability to effectively forecast the susceptibility of police officers to potential behavioral and/or emotional difficulties. In addition, the integration of biodata with the MMPI-2-RF increases the capacity to assess and identify potential emotional and behavioral concerns. Nevertheless, critics argue that the MMPI-2-RF is not devoid of its limitations   During employment evaluations, intentionally deceiving symptoms presents a major obstacle, particularly when it entails minimizing, omitting, or downplaying pathological traits, emotions, or behavioral abnormalities. A cadet who is not suitable for the position may be mistakenly administered the oath of office because of misleading or incorrect scoring. (Balthrop 2018; Corey & Ben-Porath, 2014). 
The theory of constructed emotion incorporates both the physical and mental aspects, as the components of an emotional experience arise from innate bodily rhythms (Barrett & Satpute, 2019). Sleep dysregulation, digestive issues, sicknesses, and changes in consciousness caused by substances have an effect on the perception of internal bodily sensations, emotions, and the body's ability to regulate its resources. Li et al. (2013) found that individuals with depression who died by suicide exhibit dysregulation in clock genes, which control circadian rhythm and indicate abnormalities with controlling energy. Individuals with impaired interoception, affect, and the allostatic or body budgeting system can be identified with the use of the Minnesota multiphasic personality inventory-2 and Minnesota multiphasic personality inventory-2-restructured form MMPI-2 and MMPI

The collective analysis of research related to the deployment of mental health screening within police forces underscores the significant prevalence of PTSD and related disorders among first responders, advocating for routine mental health screenings as a pivotal component in fostering a mentally resilient work environment. The utilization of screening processes aims at the early detection of mental health conditions, thereby enabling timely intervention and support. Pre-employment testing serves as a preventive measure to minimize the recruitment of individuals potentially susceptible to future mental health challenges, highlighting the critical role of screening in maintaining workforce integrity and operational effectiveness.

**MMPI-2 Screening Tool Insights**

The synthesis of findings from nine articles has illuminated the capacity of the Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory-2 (MMPI-2) as a predictive instrument for future police officer misconduct, with certain MMPI-2 scales being indicative of mental illness and psychopathology predispositions. Specific behavioral patterns leading to employment termination have been correlated with particular scores on the MMPI-2, pointing to its efficacy in identifying candidates unsuitable for police work based on psychological factors. Despite some studies questioning the direct correlation between MMPI-2 scores and mental health issues, the tool's utility in foreseeing potential behavioral and mental challenges remains recognized within the law enforcement context.

**Machine Learning and MMPI-2**

The integration of machine learning techniques with MMPI-2 demonstrates consistent and reliable outcomes in predicting suicidal ideation and attempts, marking a significant advancement in the accuracy of mental health forecasting through screening tools. However, the risk of advancing candidates with concerning profiles, due to the downplaying of negative findings, calls into question the commitment to the integrity of the selection process.

**MMPI-2-RF and MMPI-3 as Predictive Tools**

The adaptation of the MMPI-2 to the Restructured Form (MMPI-2-RF) and its subsequent evolution to MMPI-3 has broadened the scope of predictive analysis in assessing the susceptibility of police officers to emotional and behavioral problems. The MMPI-2-RF, and by extension MMPI-3, have proven successful in predicting future officer behavior, emotional challenges, and the potential for psychopathology related to personality disorders. Incorporating biodata with MMPI scores enhances the predictive accuracy, although concerns persist regarding the potential for deception or inaccurate representation within these assessments.

**Challenges and Criticisms**

The reliance on MMPI variations for screening raises ethical and practical considerations, especially regarding the potential for misleading outcomes due to symptom minimization or exaggeration. The integrity of the selection process is further complicated by the leniency in recruitment standards, potentially compromising the psychological fitness of candidates entering the force.

**Theoretical Implications**

The relevance of the theory of constructed emotion within this context underscores the complexity of emotional and psychological experiences, influenced by physiological states and environmental interactions. Dysregulation in bodily rhythms and cognitive processes, as suggested by abnormalities in clock genes among individuals with depression, highlights the intricate interplay between biological systems and mental health outcomes. The MMPI tools, in their various forms, stand as critical components in navigating this complex landscape, offering insights into the allostatic regulation and potential mental health challenges faced by law enforcement personnel.

In conclusion, the use of MMPI-based screening tools in police officer recruitment and evaluation reflects a nuanced approach to addressing the mental health challenges within law enforcement. While these tools offer valuable predictive capabilities, their effectiveness is contingent upon ethical application, a commitment to follow-through on concerning findings, and broader considerations of health and well-being that extend beyond initial screenings.

Discuss the findings:  Throughout their tenure, police officers may encounter numerous distressing incidents. Police officers exhibit significantly elevated rates of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), suicidal tendencies, and substance misuse compared to the general population. Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), trauma therapy, eye movement desensitization reprocessing (EMDR), and mindfulness are all scientifically supported methods that effectively address the mental health challenges experienced by police officers.  MDMA is a novel approach that is becoming increasingly popular for the management of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD). RQ 3 included the analysis of 36 articles. 
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy 
Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is an empirically validated psychological intervention that successfully targets a range of mental health disorders frequently observed among law enforcement personnel, including anxiety, depression, and substance abuse (Gautam et al., 2020; Moorey & Hollon, 2021; Vasile, 2020).  Research has demonstrated that anti-depressant drugs exhibit comparable efficacy to CBT in the treatment of depression. Some researchers have obtained similar results, demonstrating that both anti-depressant medication and cognitive behavioral therapy are equally beneficial in reducing symptoms of depression (Beshai et al., 2019; Dunlop et al., 2017). 
A meta-analysis findings demonstrated a notable decrease in symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder, depression, and anxiety because of psychological therapies. The data further demonstrated that CBT had significantly greater efficacy compared to alternative therapies in treating post-traumatic stress disorder (Alshahrani et al., 2022). The findings of a separate study align with previous research that confirms the effectiveness of cognitive behavioral therapy. This study saw a significant decrease in symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder after undergoing the treatment (Hassan & Holmes, 2023). 
The rising popularity of telehealth enables individuals to receive medical care from the comfort of their own residences via computer or telephone. This could be an appealing choice for the law enforcement community, which is usually focused on maintaining privacy and confidentiality. A comprehensive examination of the current computerized CBT platforms was carried out, revealing its efficacy as a remedy for low mood (Hofman & Asmundson, 2017; Lui, 2021). Supporting these studies are two meta-analyses that have been carried out, which have found that computerized CBT is equally effective compared to traditional CBT (Carlbring et al., 2017; Wright et al., 2019). Along with computerized cognitive behavioral therapy, telephone-based cognitive behavioral therapy has demonstrated potential as a successful intervention for depression (Cujipers et al., 2019; Luo et al., 2020).  
Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing 
The primary theme detected in the analysis of 9 articles indicates that eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR) is a highly successful treatment for depression, anxiety, and symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder. EMDR therapy comprehensively considers the officer's necessities across their clinically significant interpersonal, emotional, and behavioral aspects. The objective is to assist with surmounting the challenges arising from trauma and promoting their individual growth and advancement (Laliotis et al., 2021). EMDR therapy has established a robust evidence base as a major treatment for PTSD. Studies have consistently demonstrated its effectiveness in persons suffering from this debilitating mental health disease (de Jongh et al., 2019; Laugharne et al., 2016; Van den Berg et al., 2015). 
Based on research, it has been determined that eye movement desensitization and reprocessing is an effective approach for reducing the symptoms associated with work-related trauma among first responders (Morris et al., 2022). The findings of this study are corroborated by other studies suggesting that it could be an effective method for addressing post-traumatic stress disorder, anxiety, and depression in first responders who incur these symptoms while on the job (Jarero et al., 2019; Torchalla & Strehlau, 2018). The findings of a meta-analysis provide evidence in favor of utilizing eye movement desensitization and reprocessing as a potentially effective method for addressing depression (Dominguez et al., 2021). 
Eye movement desensitization and reprocessing has been around for over 30 years. Based on the research conducted by Torchalla and Strehlau in 2018, eye movement desensitization and reprocessing is effective in reducing symptoms of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and helping police personnel resume their duties. EMDR treatment has evolved over several decades from a simple strategy of reducing sensitivity to treat posttraumatic stress disorder to a comprehensive psychotherapy approach that tackles a wide range of clinical issues in various contexts and with different types of clients (Laliotis et al., 2021). 
Mindfulness for Anxiety and Depression
The examination of 11 articles revealed the theme that mindfulness is a highly effective method for alleviating symptoms of stress, anxiety, and depression. Mindfulness can be defined as an intricate process that involves the cooperation of multiple interconnected cognitive mechanisms that mutually enhance and reinforce each other. The key mental function involved in this process is the capacity to focus on the present moment, encompassing one's internal cognitive processes, emotions, physical sensations, behaviors, and the surrounding outside atmosphere. Mindfulness requires taking a focused and empathetic stance, which reduces or eliminates automatic reactions to the current moment and subsequent assessments, predictions, analyses, and criticisms of what is happening or has occurred (Ribeiro et al., 2019). 
Research provides substantial evidence that engaging in mindfulness practice results in long-lasting enhancements in an individual's brain functioning, emotional well-being, and behavioral patterns (Hansen et al., 2016; Ruffault et al., 2017). A recent study revealed that mindfulness meditation yielded a notable stress-alleviating impact on police officers. It unveiled a decline in the participants' hostility and stress levels, an enhancement in their emotional state, and a boost in their capacity to regulate their emotions. There is increasing data indicating that police officers who engage in mindfulness practices may help reduce the mental and physical impacts of stress (Ribeiro et al., 2019; Fisher et al., 2019; Kaplan et al., 2018).  
Other studies have supported the efficacy of mindfulness for the treatment of ailments such as anxiety, depression, burnout, and PTSD. Initial data indicate that mindfulness may also be advantageous in alleviating depressed symptoms (Khatib et al., 2022; Krick & Felfe, 2019). Studies were conducted to assess the effects of mindfulness on law enforcement officers. Enhancements were observed in domains such as fatigue, sleep quality, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder (Christopher et al., 2018; Grupe et al., 2019; Hoeve et al., 2021).
Methylenedioxymethamphetamine for Post-traumatic Stress Disorder 
Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is prevalent among the police population in the United States. After analyzing 10 articles, it was determined that Methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA) shows promise as a viable method for relieving symptoms associated with post-traumatic stress disorder. Recent studies have demonstrated that MDMA is a potential and innovative therapeutic drug for the treatment of ptsd. Before being classified as a restricted substance, mental health experts utilized it to address a variety of psychiatric conditions. Recently, MDMA has emerged as a promising treatment for ptsd and the results from randomized, controlled trials support its use as an effective pharmacological therapy when combined with psychotherapy (Latimer et al., 2021). 
Multiple phase II clinical trials were conducted on MDMA-assisted therapy for post-traumatic stress disorder. The participants, who had received a diagnosis of PTSD, did not observe any alteration in symptoms following a minimum of three months of antidepressant medication and six months of psychotherapy. Following only two sessions of MDMA-assisted therapy, slightly more than half of the participants no longer exhibited symptoms severe enough to warrant a diagnosis of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) (Mithoefer et al., 2019). Following a subsequent session, the quantity rose to two-thirds (Mitchell et al., 2021). The number exhibited stability during the 1-year follow-up period (Jerome et al., 2020). The figure rose to almost 75% four years following the conclusion of the MDMA therapy sessions. 
The commencement of Phase III studies took place in 2021, yielding outcomes that were comparable to those observed in Phase II. The analysis of Phase III indicates that MDMA-assisted therapy is highly effective, secure, and well-received by participants.  MDMA-assisted therapy holds significant promise as a transformative approach in the management of ptsd (Mitchell et al., 2021). Additional studies have demonstrated that the administration of MDMA to a group of 90 persons experiencing persistent post-traumatic stress disorder not only diminished the intensity of their symptoms but also enhanced their self-perception (Slomski, 2021). A study indicates that MDMA-assisted therapy carries a little risk of adverse effects, yet its benefit on depression symptoms is only moderate (Smith et al., 2022). This meta-analysis contributes more support to the increasing amount of research indicating the possibility of MDMA-assisted therapy in reducing symptoms of PTSD (Illingsworth et al., 2021). A supplementary meta-analysis has determined that MDMA-assisted therapy could be advantageous for those suffering from chronic, unresponsive post-traumatic stress disorder (Bahji, 2020).
Discussion Research Question Three

	The analysis of several approaches to treating mental health conditions routinely faced by 
law enforcement officers, cognitive behavioral therapy, eye movement desensitization 
and reprocessing, and mindfulness was conducted through evidence synthesis. The findings demonstrate the high efficacy of each method in addressing the prevalent conditions experienced by the police population, including anxiety, depression, stress, and post-traumatic stress disorder. An additional method, Methylenedioxymethamphetamine, has demonstrated significant potential in the management of post-traumatic stress disorder as indicated by the results of clinical trials. 
Cognitive behavioral therapy has shown effectiveness in treating the mental health conditions commonly associated with the police profession but is also very accessible in that it is equally effective as a remote treatment via the computer. The notion the treatment can be conducted via a computer within the comfort of one's own residence should significantly alleviate the anxiety associated with avoiding visibility and desiring anonymous treatment. Findings from this study point to eye movement desensitization and reprocessing as a potential efficient and effective method for treating post-traumatic stress disorder, anxiety, and depression in first responders who experience these conditions on the job. This evidence synthesis found strong evidence that EMDR therapy is equally effective as the established standard for trauma treatment, cognitive behavioral therapy and is a more economical intervention (de Jongh et al., 2019; Laugharne et al., 2016; Van den Berg et al., 2015). 
There is considerable evidence from research that practicing mindfulness leads to enduring improvements in a person's brain, emotions, and behaviors. Mindfulness is the deliberate cognitive process of directing one's attention to a specific object of perception in the current moment, without making evaluative judgments (Kabat-zin, 1990). The evidence synthesis findings demonstrate that mindfulness yields positive effects by reducing aggression, enhancing mood, promoting self-awareness and self-care, and mitigating the physiological and psychological impacts of stress. Research provides substantial evidence that engaging in mindfulness practice results in long-lasting enhancements in an individual's brain function, emotional well-being, and behavioral patterns (de vibe et al., 2012; Hansen et al., 2016; Ruffault et al., 2017). 
MDMA-assisted therapy is a recently developed treatment modality that exhibits potential. It is rapidly gaining traction in clinical trials and is expected by many to be a transformative treatment for individuals with post-traumatic stress disorder. According to the research, MDMA-assisted therapy contributes to a quick and effective treatment response to those with severe post-traumatic stress disorder and other related conditions such as depression, alcohol and substance use, and childhood trauma. MDMA-assisted therapy is effective in treating those suffering from severe PTSD and may also enhance safety. When compared to traditional treatment therapies, MDMA-assisted therapy has an ability to significantly revolutionize the treatment of post-traumatic stress disorder and should be promptly assessed for clinical application.
The theory of constructive emotion posits that interoception is a fundamental component of the brain's internal model and is derived from the function of allostasis (Barrett, 2017). Allostasis is not a state of the body, but rather a mechanism by which the brain controls the body based on the advantages and disadvantages involved. To achieve optimal performance, the brain must be able to predict the body's requirements and fulfill them proactively. According to the theory of constructive emotion, it is not a mere coincidence that the most effective therapies, such as cognitive behavioral therapy, eye movement desensitization reprocessing, mindfulness, and MDMA, all depend on achieving a state of equilibrium in the brain, body, and mind. 
According to the theory of constructive emotion, the primary function of the brain is to predict and respond to the metabolic requirements of the body by initiating actions to address those needs in advance. The brain maintains this process of allostasis by constantly forming a representation of the internal condition of the body and the external environment. The utilization of metabolic resources that have not yet been replenished might lead to distress and tiredness. Individuals experiencing shifts in mood are affected by disruptions in energy regulation due to the brain's predictive modeling of the body (Shaffer et al., 2022). Cognitive behavioral therapy and eye movement desensitization and reprocessing therapy are highly efficacious in addressing depression by facilitating the modification of their predictions, conceivably by enhancing their ability to process prediction errors and improve understanding of concepts (Barrett et al., 2016). According to the theory of constructed emotion, mindfulness practice works to mitigate the effect of allostasis overload. Mindfulness is an effective way to regulate the energy imbalance that is caused by stressful encounters by mitigating the effects of the allostasis overload. Also, previous research has demonstrated that mindfulness has the potential to enhance an individual's long-term capacity to focus on interoceptive experiences (Farb et al., 2013).

The comprehensive analysis of 36 articles has illuminated the dire mental health landscape encountered by police officers throughout their careers, marked by a higher prevalence of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), suicidal tendencies, and substance misuse than observed within the general population. The body of research reviewed underscores the efficacy of various therapeutic interventions aimed at ameliorating the psychological burdens borne by these first responders. Among these, Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT), Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR), mindfulness practices, and the innovative application of Methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA) emerge as potent modalities for addressing the complex mental health challenges endemic within law enforcement populations.

**Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT)**

CBT stands distinguished as a well-validated psychological intervention, demonstrating notable success in mitigating symptoms of anxiety, depression, and substance abuse prevalent among police officers. The equivalency in efficacy between antidepressant medications and CBT in treating depression further underscores CBT's value. Moreover, the advent of telehealth and computerized platforms for delivering CBT offers a significant advance, potentially enhancing accessibility and acceptance of mental health care within the police community, concerned with privacy and confidentiality.

**Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR)**

EMDR therapy, with its robust evidence base in treating PTSD, shows considerable promise for law enforcement personnel. Its success in addressing work-related trauma and facilitating officers' return to duty highlights its relevance and potential as a critical resource in the psychological toolkit available to first responders. This therapeutic approach not only targets PTSD symptoms but also encourages holistic personal growth and coping skills development among affected individuals.

**Mindfulness Practices**

Mindfulness practices are identified as effective interventions for reducing stress, anxiety, and depression symptoms. The ability of mindfulness to foster a heightened state of present-moment awareness and non-judgmental acceptance contributes to lasting improvements in brain function, emotional regulation, and behavior patterns. Such practices are especially conducive to law enforcement officers, potentially diminishing the adverse mental and physical effects of occupational stress.

**Methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA)-assisted Therapy**

MDMA-assisted therapy represents a novel and intriguing treatment avenue for PTSD, showcasing significant effectiveness in clinical trials. This approach may offer profound and rapid relief for officers grappling with severe PTSD, representing a paradigm shift in the treatment landscape for stress-related disorders within this demographic. Despite its potential, the application of MDMA-assisted therapy warrants careful consideration and further research to fully understand its benefits and risks.

**Implications of Findings**

The convergence of findings from the reviewed articles points to a multifaceted approach to addressing the mental health crisis among police officers. The array of interventions ranging from conventional therapies like CBT and EMDR to innovative practices such as mindfulness and MDMA-assisted therapy, highlights the plurality of effective treatments available. Each modality brings a unique set of tools for coping with the psychological demands inherent to law enforcement work, offering hope for enhanced mental health outcomes for affected personnel.

**Theoretical Insights**

The theory of constructed emotion provides a compelling framework for understanding these interventions' effectiveness. It suggests that therapies like CBT, EMDR, mindfulness, and MDMA-assisted therapy may work by restoring a state of equilibrium within the brain's predictive and allostatic mechanisms. By aiding individuals in recalibrating their internal models and predictions about stress and trauma, these treatments facilitate a more harmonious alignment between cognitive processes, bodily states, and emotional well-being.

In conclusion, the manifold challenges encountered by police officers necessitate a dynamic and responsive mental health care paradigm. The evidence underscores the critical need for accessible, effective, and diverse treatment modalities to address the complex spectrum of mental health issues within this population. As the field evolves, ongoing research and adaptation of therapeutic interventions will be paramount in ensuring the well-being and functional efficacy of those who serve and protect our communities.

Discuss the findings: Results Research Question Four
Psychological illnesses are common within the law enforcement population, but many police officers seem hesitant to seek treatment for mental health issues. Considering the widespread occurrence and significant consequences of poor mental health within the field of law enforcement, it is imperative to identify methods to enhance the utilization of mental health resources available to police officers. The primary obstacles that impede police officers from getting mental health therapy are stigma, police culture, and confidentiality. All three obstacles contribute to the apprehension over the repercussions of having a mental health disorder. RQ 4 included the analysis of 24 articles. 
Police Officer Mental Healthcare Stigma
The comprehensive analysis of 8 articles determined that the presence of stigma presents a significant obstacle for police officers seeking mental health care. Police officers and members are particularly susceptible to the detrimental impacts of stigma. Up to 33% of police officers have experienced the adverse consequences of stigma surrounding mental health concerns (Haugen et al., 2017; Wheeler et al., 2018). Moreover, police officers have a higher propensity to embrace stigma, possess a pessimistic perspective about rehabilitation, and refrain from seeking aid (Soomro & Yanos, 2019). 
The results indicate that within the law enforcement population, there is a prevailing fear of discussing or seeking help for mental health issues. This is due to the perception that such conditions are grounds for disqualification from a career in law enforcement (Bell & Palmer-Conn, 2018; Caitlin et al., 2022; Haugen et al., 2017; Jetelina et al., 2020; Richards et al., 2021). Police officers may exhibit reluctance in seeking assistance due to apprehensions over confidentiality, which is reasonable considering the pervasive social disapproval associated with mental health intervention (Bell & Palmer-Conn, 2018; Caitlin et al., 2022; Haugen et al., 2017; Jetelina et al., 2020; Richards et al., 2021). 
The primary difficulties to seeking therapy were identified as the fear of being viewed as weak or unfit for law enforcement job, concerns about jeopardizing one's professional prospects, and the impact of cultural stigmatization on psychological services (Drew & Martin, 2021; Haugen et al., 2017; Soomro & Yanos, 2019; Wheeler et al., 2018). Drew and Marin (2021) concluded a significant number of law enforcement officers suffering from mental illnesses are not receiving adequate psychological care due to the negative social perception around such conditions. The study revealed that even police officers who received mental health therapy expressed a significant degree of ambivalence due to the associated stigma. The results of another study align closely with the outcomes of prior studies, which identified factors such as reluctance to reveal mental struggles, apprehension regarding societal attitudes towards individuals with mental disorders, and resentment towards those who choose to pursue treatment or receive a clinical diagnosis (Burzee et al., 2022).
Police Officer Subculture 
The exhaustive examination of 10 articles concluded that police officers demonstrate resistance in seeking mental health services as a result of the impact of the police subculture. Efficient law enforcement is crucial for diminishing criminal activity and guaranteeing the well-being of the general population. Police personnel frequently encounter distressing occurrences as they carry out their duties. Lack of sufficient assistance can result in a decline in the mental well-being of a police officer when exposed to such situations for an extended period. Due to the prevailing police subculture, particularly the adverse views surrounding accepting treatment for mental health issues, numerous police officers experience distress in solitude, further contributing to their emotional decline. 
	Although mental illness is common in the law enforcement population, the police culture has historically been unwilling to embrace interventions aimed at addressing this issue. Although the mental well-being of a police officer is contingent upon the subculture and leadership style inside their organization (Deschenes et al., 2018), police officers are far from feeling at ease with seeking and obtaining therapy because of the shared norms and beliefs within the police organization (Cohen et al., 2019). Law enforcement personnel frequently exhibit reluctance in seeking assistance for matters pertaining to their mental health (Ricciardelli et al., 2020; Watson & Andrews, 2018).  A study conducted by Carleton et al. (2020) reveals that more than 50% of law enforcement officers rarely undergo any treatment. 
The phenomenon of the 'cult of masculinity' has garnered much scrutiny as an enduring and detrimental element of police culture (Silversti, 2017). The perception of mental health issues within law enforcement agencies is significantly shaped by traditional machismo attitudes, leading to a stigma. The police force is primarily composed of males and exhibits a resilient cultural image. The concept of mental illness as a vulnerability is prevalent among law enforcement personnel (Bell & Eski, 2016; Velazquez & Hernandez, 2019). 
Studies have revealed a strong correlation between cultural biases against mental illness and the law enforcement subculture, resulting in a significant prevalence of structural stigma. The traditional obstacles faced by police officers in pursuing therapy entail the negative perception and prevailing mindset within law enforcement that dissuades attempts towards mental well-being (Rodriguez et al., 2023). The culture inside the police force is marked by a negative perception towards seeking treatment, apprehensions about the boundaries of confidentiality, and doubts about the pertinence and effectiveness of available treatment options 
(Rodriguez et al., 2023; White et al., 2016). 
Discussion Research Question Four
	The prevalence of stigma within the law enforcement community is highly consequential. The law enforcement community exhibits a heightened demand for mental health intervention, yet concurrently faces a more pronounced prevalence of cultural disapproval in comparison to the general population. Police officers experience heightened anxiety due to the existence of stigma, as they are concerned about the potential breach of confidentiality and the negative impact on their professional standing.  It is imperative to undergo a significant transformation in the attitudes of police officers towards seeking treatment to reduce the stigma. 
The existence of the police subculture contributes to the perpetuation of the negative perception associated with mental health treatment. Despite the prevalence of mental illness among law enforcement personnel, the police culture has traditionally shown reluctance in accepting treatments designed to tackle this problem. Altering the risk associated with being a law enforcement officer and the likelihood of developing a mental illness due to the cumulative stress and trauma will pose significant challenges. The subculture that disapproves of seeking help will likely be less challenging to change. 
The prevailing stigma is partly attributed to the perspectives within the police subculture. A shift in police subculture will be imperative to diminish the number of police officers afflicted with mental illness who are responsible for maintaining societal safety. The profound impact of stigma as a barrier to accessing mental health resources among police officers calls for deliberate, sustained efforts to dismantle misconceptions surrounding mental health within the law enforcement community.
